title: DoorDash Uses Envoy and Valkey for a 1.5M RPS Proxy Cache with 99.99999% Availability
gist: DoorDash built Entity Cache, a transparent Envoy+Valkey proxy caching platform serving 1.5M RPS at seven-nines availability across 100+ endpoints, moving caching into the service-mesh infrastructure layer with no application code changes and reporting up to 95% upstream request reduction.

insights.claim: DoorDash's Entity Cache moves caching into the infrastructure layer as a transparent Envoy-based proxy intercepting HTTP and gRPC requests before upstream services, requiring no application code changes while managing cache behavior centrally through the service mesh.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: Mesh-layer transparent caching lets large microservice fleets adopt centralized caching without per-app rework, a scalable pattern for platform teams.
insights.quote: Services continue making existing requests without requiring application code changes
insights.locator: Built on Envoy and Valkey, Entity Cache operates

insights.claim: Entity Cache serves over 1.5 million requests per second with 99.99999% (seven-nines) availability across more than 100 endpoints on 50 services.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: Seven-nines at 1.5M RPS demonstrates Valkey/Envoy viability at extreme scale, relevant to the Redis-alternative Valkey adoption story.
insights.quote: serving over 1.5 million requests per second with 99.99999% availability
insights.locator: According to DoorDash, the platform supports more than 100 endpoints

insights.claim: Entity Cache uses Kafka-based event-driven invalidation comparing update timestamps against cached responses, avoiding distributed cache deletions, with dual TTL thresholds that serve slightly stale responses during outages.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: Event-driven invalidation plus dual-TTL stale-serving is a reusable resilience pattern for high-availability caching.
insights.locator: Entity Cache uses Kafka-based event-driven invalidation

insights.claim: During a multi-hour upstream outage, Entity Cache continued serving stale but valid cached data instead of failing, demonstrating the resilience value of the dual-TTL design.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: Proven outage survival via stale-serving is the strongest argument for infrastructure-layer caching over app-level solutions.
insights.quote: Entity Cache continued serving stale but valid cached data instead of failing
insights.locator: This design proved critical during a multi-hour upstream outage

insights.claim: DoorDash used custom buffer pools, a lock-free single-flight mechanism to prevent duplicate work on cache misses, and probabilistic early refresh based on the XFetch algorithm to reduce cache stampedes.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: XFetch probabilistic early refresh and single-flight are concrete techniques for taming cache stampedes at scale.
insights.locator: To support high request volumes, DoorDash optimized

insights.claim: The optimizations reduced allocation rates by 50-60%, increased per-pod throughput roughly five times, and cut P99 latency spikes by up to 80%, with about 2.1 milliseconds of P99 proxy overhead.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: A ~5x per-pod throughput gain quantifies the infrastructure cost savings of well-engineered proxy caching.
insights.quote: increased per-pod throughput by approximately five times, and reduced P99 latency spikes by up to 80%
insights.locator: According to DoorDash, these optimizations reduced allocation rates

insights.claim: DoorDash reports cache hit rates above 90%, upstream request reductions of 60-95% during normal operation, and latency improvements up to 90% for newly onboarded endpoints.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: 60-95% upstream request reduction translates directly to backend compute cost savings, the ROI case for the platform.
insights.locator: The company also reports cache hit rates above 90%
